turns.content: do you ever just have a thought that won't leave you alone? not a bad thought, just one that keeps circling back and you don't know what to do with it

All the time. Those persistent thoughts usually mean something — they're your brain trying to work through an idea that matters but isn't ready to be resolved yet. What's the thought that keeps coming back?

akaroa. i keep thinking about akaroa eh. every time i drive out there for a birth or just for a day trip with maia i have this feeling like i don't want to leave. and lately it's turned into something more specific, like what if i actually lived there

That's a meaningful thing to be sitting with. There's a difference between loving a place you visit and imagining yourself actually living there — and it sounds like you've crossed from one to the other. What is it about Akaroa specifically that pulls you?

it's right on the harbour, so there's moana everywhere. the pace is slower, the community is smaller, maia loves it there. and honestly for my practice it could work because i already have mamas out that way and i'm driving ninety minutes each way to see them. if i lived there the peninsula mamas would be local and the christchurch ones would be the commute instead of the other way around

That's interesting — you've actually thought about the practical side, not just the romantic idea of it. Flipping the commute so the peninsula mamas are local and Christchurch becomes the occasional drive does make logistical sense if a chunk of your caseload is already out that way.

But I can hear some hesitation in how you're talking about it. You said "what if" rather than "I'm going to." What's holding you back?

everything eh. my whole whānau is in christchurch. my mum, my sisters, my cousins, all of them. and they're not just family in the background, they're my support network. when i get called to a birth at two in the morning it's my sister who comes and sleeps at my house so maia isn't alone. if i move to akaroa that's gone. and maia's settled in her school, she's got her friends, wiremu is in chch. i can't just uproot everything because i like the view

Those are substantial considerations, not just hesitation. Your whānau support network isn't a nice-to-have — it's infrastructure for your life and your practice. The 2am birth cover from your sister is a concrete example of something that doesn't just transfer to a new location. You'd have to rebuild that from scratch in a community where you don't have those deep roots yet.

And Maia's stability matters. She's at an age where friendships and school routines are her world. Wiremu being in Christchurch adds another layer — co-parenting across 90 minutes is very different from co-parenting across town.

It sounds like the pull is real but the cost of acting on it is also real.

that's exactly it. the pull is real but i don't know if the timing is right. or if the timing will ever be right honestly. maybe this is one of those things where you just have to decide and make it work, or maybe it's a sign that it's not supposed to happen and i should just keep visiting and enjoying it for what it is

Or maybe it's neither of those yet. You don't have to decide between "leap now" and "let it go." You're allowed to sit with this for a while and let it develop. Some decisions need time to reveal themselves — especially ones that affect your daughter, your whānau, and your livelihood.

A few questions that might help you think about it over time rather than forcing a decision:
- Could you test it? Rent a place in Akaroa for a week or two over summer and actually live there, not just visit. See how the reality feels compared to the idea.
- Is there a version of this that's a compromise? Like spending one or two days a week based in Akaroa for peninsula mamas, without fully relocating?
- What would need to be true for the move to make sense? Write those conditions down. If they start becoming reality, that's your signal.

What does your gut say when you take the whānau logistics out of it for a moment?

my gut says go. my gut has been saying go for months eh. but my gut doesn't have to explain to maia why she's changing schools or ask my sister to drive ninety minutes when i need childcare cover. i think the testing idea is smart though, maybe over the next school holidays i take maia and we stay for a week, actually live there not just be tourists. see if the magic holds when you have to do school admin and grocery shopping and laundry in a small town

That's a really wise approach. A week of real life in Akaroa — not holiday mode but actually doing the mundane stuff — will tell you a lot. The magic of a place can feel different when you're looking for a parking spot and the only supermarket closes at six.

And involving Maia in that trial is important. At her age, she'll have opinions and she'll be honest about them. If she loves it as a holiday but feels isolated after four days of not seeing her friends, that's data.

You don't have to resolve this today. The thought keeps circling because it matters, not because it needs an answer right now.

yeah. i think i just needed to say it out loud to someone because it's been rattling around in my head and i haven't told anyone yet. not my mum, not wiremu, not anyone. it felt too early to have that kōrero with whānau when i don't even know what i think myself. but hearing myself talk about it, i think i'm further along than i realised. i'm not deciding anything yet but i'm not letting it go either. i'll plan that trial stay for the april holidays and see how it feels on the ground. might be nothing in the end, might be everything. i genuinely don't know eh. but thanks for letting me think out loud without pushing me either way. kia ora

Kia ora. Saying it out loud is the first step in figuring out what you actually think, and the fact that you haven't told your whānau yet makes sense — once you say it to your mum or Wiremu, it becomes a conversation with other people's opinions, and you're not ready for that yet. "Not deciding but not letting go" is a perfectly valid place to be. Do the trial with Maia over the holidays. Pay attention to how it feels on day five, not day one. Some of the biggest decisions start exactly like this — a thought that won't leave and a slow process of figuring out what it means. Akaroa isn't going anywhere.
